flashcards.front: What is Replication in experimental design?
flashcards.back: Replication is the practice of performing the same test or measurement under identical conditions more than once. It ensures consistency and reliability of results. For example, running a sorting algorithm on 50 different instances of the same dataset to check for variability in performance.

flashcards.front: What is Randomization and why is it important in experiments?
flashcards.back: Randomization is the process of assigning experimental units (such as subjects or datasets) to different treatment groups using random procedures. It is important because it eliminates systematic bias and ensures that each treatment group is representative of the population, making results more valid.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between single-blinding and double-blinding?
flashcards.back: In single-blinding, only the subjects are unaware of which experimental group they belong to. In double-blinding, both the subjects and the experimenters are unaware of group assignments. Double-blinding provides stronger protection against bias than single-blinding.

flashcards.front: What is the role of a Control Group in an experiment?
flashcards.back: A control group provides a baseline for comparison. It receives no treatment or a standard treatment. By comparing experimental group results against the control group, researchers can determine whether the treatment had a real effect. For example, using QuickSort as a baseline when evaluating new sorting algorithms.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between One-Way and Two-Way Factorial Designs?
flashcards.back: A One-Way Factorial Design studies only one factor (independent variable) at a time. A Two-Way Factorial Design examines two factors simultaneously and also analyzes their interaction effects. Two-way designs are more efficient when multiple factors may influence the outcome.

flashcards.front: How is ANOVA used in the context of experimental design?
flashcards.back: ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) is a statistical method used to compare means across multiple groups to determine if the differences are statistically significant. In experimental design, it helps analyze results -- for example, determining whether four sorting algorithms have significantly different performance levels. Post-hoc tests like Tukey's HSD can then identify which specific pairs differ.
